title: Code Minifier
description: Minify JavaScript, CSS, and HTML online for free. Removes comments and collapses whitespace to reduce file size — no external packages, runs entirely in your browser.
empty: No output — check if your input is valid.

placeholder.js: Paste your JavaScript or TypeScript code here...

note.js: Light minification: comments removed, whitespace collapsed. Variable renaming and dead-code elimination are not performed.
note.css: Comments removed, whitespace collapsed. url() values and custom properties are preserved.
note.html: Comments removed, whitespace between tags collapsed. Content inside <pre>, <script>, and <style> tags is not handled specially.

howToUse.label: Select the language
howToUse.description: Click JS, CSS, or HTML to choose the type of code you want to minify.

howToUse.label: Paste your code
howToUse.description: Paste the source code into the input area. The minified output appears automatically below.

howToUse.label: Copy the result
howToUse.description: Click "Copy" to copy the minified code. The reduction percentage shows how much smaller the output is.

faq.question: What does this minifier do?
faq.answer: It removes comments (// and /* */ for JS, /* */ for CSS, <!-- --> for HTML) and collapses multiple whitespace characters into a single space. It does not rename variables, tree-shake, or perform advanced optimizations.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on production code?
faq.answer: For CSS and HTML, yes — the transformations are safe. For JavaScript, the light minification is generally safe, but avoid using it as a replacement for a full bundler (Webpack, Vite, etc.) in production. Template literals with complex nested expressions may not be handled correctly.

faq.question: Why is the reduction percentage low?
faq.answer: If your code has few comments and is already compactly written, the reduction will be small. This tool only removes whitespace and comments — it cannot rename variables or apply advanced compression.

faq.question: Does minified code run the same as the original?
faq.answer: In almost all cases, yes. The exceptions are JavaScript relying on comment-based annotations (e.g., some Angular/legacy build tools) or HTML where whitespace inside <pre> is significant.
